does: This paper introduces SG-Bench, a comprehensive safety benchmark that evaluates large language models across both generative and discriminative tasks while systematically varying prompt engineering techniques. The benchmark incorporates diverse evaluation paradigms—including standard prompts, system prompts, few-shot demonstrations, and chain-of-thought reasoning—to measure how safety alignment generalizes under different conditions. Testing 13 LLMs (3 proprietary and 10 open-source), the authors document significant disparities in safety performance, showing that models typically perform worse on discriminative tasks than generative ones and exhibit substantial vulnerability to prompt variations, indicating fragile safety alignment that fails to generalize robustly across practical usage scenarios.
connects: This work addresses fundamental gaps in existing LLM safety evaluation frameworks, which have separately developed discriminative benchmarks (like standard classification tasks) and generative benchmarks (like open-ended response generation) without examining their relationship or interaction. It builds on prior work in adversarial robustness testing and jailbreaking research by systematizing how prompting techniques—widely used in practice but underexplored in safety evaluation—degrade model safety. The research extends beyond static benchmarks by incorporating realistic prompt engineering variations, responding to the gap between controlled safety evaluations and how users actually deploy these models through system prompts and in-context learning.
enables: This benchmark and its findings enable future work on more robust safety alignment techniques that generalize across evaluation paradigms and prompt variations. It provides empirical motivation for developing safety training methods resilient to prompt engineering and few-shot learning, moving beyond current approaches that optimize for narrow evaluation scenarios. The systematic evaluation framework facilitates comparative studies of safety alignment strategies and jailbreak mitigation. These results also support the development of safety evaluation protocols that better reflect real-world deployment conditions, encouraging research into understanding the mechanistic roots of safety generalization failures and designing alignment approaches that maintain integrity across diverse prompting patterns and task formulations.
